era_context: 1715 marks a pivotal year in early 18th-century European history, dominated by the conclusion of the War of Spanish Succession and the Treaty of Utrecht (1713–1714), which fundamentally reshaped European power dynamics. The death of Louis XIV of France in September 1715 ended an era of French dominance and initiated the Regency period. Simultaneously, the Jacobite Rising in Scotland challenged the newly established Hanoverian succession in Britain. In Asia, the Kangxi Emperor continued his reign over a prosperous Qing China, while the Ottoman Empire faced mounting pressures from European powers.

events.title: Jacobite Rising of 1715 (The Fifteen)
events.description: The Jacobite Rising of 1715, known as 'The Fifteen,' was an armed rebellion launched in September 1715 to restore James Francis Edward Stuart (the Old Pretender) to the British throne, challenging the newly established Hanoverian succession of George I. Led by John Erskine, Earl of Mar, the rising began in the Scottish Highlands and attracted significant support from Scottish clans and English Jacobites. The rebellion reached its climax at the Battle of Sheriffmuir (November 13, 1715), which was tactically inconclusive but strategically a defeat for the Jacobites, as they failed to capitalize on their initial momentum. The rising was effectively suppressed by early 1716, though it demonstrated the fragility of the Hanoverian settlement and the persistence of Stuart loyalism in Scotland and northern England.
key_figures: John Erskine, Earl of Mar (Jacobite commander)
James Francis Edward Stuart (the Old Pretender)
John Campbell, Duke of Argyll (government commander)

sources.name: Contemporary government dispatches and military records

events.certainty_note: The Jacobite Rising of 1715 is extensively documented in contemporary government records, military dispatches, and Jacobite correspondence. Dates and major events are well-established.

events.title: Death of Louis XIV of France
events.description: Louis XIV, the 'Sun King' and the longest-reigning European monarch, died on September 1, 1715, at the age of 76 after a reign of 72 years (1643–1715). His death marked the end of an era of French cultural and political dominance in Europe. The succession passed to his great-grandson, Louis XV, who was only five years old, necessitating a regency under Philippe II, Duke of Orléans. The transition from Louis XIV's absolute monarchy to the Regency period (1715–1723) initiated significant changes in French foreign policy, court culture, and political dynamics, including the relaxation of some of Louis XIV's rigid court etiquette and the rise of new factions at court.

sources.name: French court records and official documentation
Memoirs of Saint-Simon and other courtiers

events.certainty_note: Louis XIV's death is one of the most thoroughly documented events in European history, with precise dating and extensive contemporary accounts.

events.title: Establishment of the Regency in France under Philippe II, Duke of Orléans
events.description: Following Louis XIV's death in September 1715, Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, was appointed regent for the young Louis XV. The Regency period (1715–1723) marked a significant departure from Louis XIV's policies. Philippe reversed many of Louis XIV's foreign policy decisions, including reconciliation with the Papacy and a shift away from aggressive expansionism. Domestically, the Regency saw the rise of the parlements (judicial bodies) in political influence, the relaxation of court ceremonialism, and increased factional competition among the nobility. The period is often characterized as one of financial instability, moral laxity (in the eyes of contemporary moralists), and cultural transition from the rigid formality of Louis XIV's court to the more libertine atmosphere of the Regency.
events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Regency is documented in French official records with precise dating and extensive contemporary accounts.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's health decline and succession concerns in Qing China
events.description: In 1715, the Kangxi Emperor, who had ruled the Qing Dynasty for over 60 years, began to show signs of declining health, though he would not die until 1722. This period saw increasing factional competition among imperial princes and court officials regarding the succession. The Kangxi Emperor's favorite son, the Crown Prince Yinreng, had been deposed in 1712 due to mental illness and political opposition, creating uncertainty about the succession. The year 1715 marked a period of heightened political tension at the Qing court as various factions maneuvered for influence and position in anticipation of the succession crisis that would follow the emperor's death.
key_figures: Kangxi's various sons and their supporters

sources.name: Qing imperial records and court documents
Memoirs and records of court officials

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's health decline in 1715 is documented in Qing records, though the precise timing and severity of his illness are somewhat obscured by court protocols that discouraged public discussion of imperial health.
cross_references: 1722-001 (Death of Kangxi Emperor and succession of Yongzheng)

events.title: Yamasee War in colonial South Carolina and Georgia
events.region: Colonial North America, South Carolina, Georgia
events.description: The Yamasee War (1715–1717) began in April 1715 when the Yamasee people and allied Native American nations launched a coordinated uprising against English colonial settlements in South Carolina and Georgia. The war was triggered by colonial encroachment on Native American lands, unfair trading practices, and the enslavement of Native Americans. The initial Yamasee offensive was devastating, killing hundreds of colonists and forcing the abandonment of outlying settlements. However, by late 1715, colonial forces, aided by Cherokee allies, began to contain the uprising. The war would continue into 1717 but marked a turning point in colonial-Native American relations, demonstrating both Native American military capability and the ultimate superiority of colonial military organization and resources.
key_figures: Yamasee leaders (names not well-documented in English sources)

sources.name: Colonial government records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The Yamasee War is well-documented in colonial records, though Native American perspectives are largely absent from surviving sources, which are predominantly English-language colonial documents.

events.title: Tokugawa Tsunayoshi's death and succession of Tokugawa Ienobu in Japan
events.description: Tokugawa Tsunayoshi, the fifth Tokugawa shogun, died on February 19, 1715, after a reign of 29 years (1686–1715). He was succeeded by his adopted son, Tokugawa Ienobu, who became the sixth shogun. Tsunayoshi is remembered for his cultural patronage and his controversial 'Edicts on Compassion for Living Things' (Shōrui Awaremi no Rei), which restricted the killing of animals and were seen by many as economically damaging. Ienobu's succession marked a shift toward more pragmatic governance and the beginning of the Kyōhō Reforms (which would be fully implemented under his successor, Tokugawa Yoshimune). The transition reflected changing priorities within the Tokugawa bakufu from cultural patronage toward administrative efficiency and fiscal responsibility.

sources.name: Tokugawa bakufu records and official documents

events.certainty_note: The succession of Tokugawa Ienobu is precisely documented in Japanese official records with exact dates.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jacobite Rising of 1715 (1715-001) is sometimes portrayed as a near-success that came close to overthrowing the Hanoverian succession. However, modern scholarship emphasizes that the rising was poorly coordinated, lacked sufficient resources, and faced a well-organized government response. The Battle of Sheriffmuir, while tactically inconclusive, was strategically decisive in preventing Jacobite momentum. Additionally, the extent of popular support for the Jacobite cause has been somewhat exaggerated in romantic historiography; many Scots and English remained loyal to the Hanoverian settlement. The Yamasee War (1715-005) is sometimes presented as a unified Native American uprising, but recent scholarship emphasizes the diversity of Native American nations involved and the complex, sometimes conflicting motivations of different groups. The characterization of Tokugawa Tsunayoshi's reign as a period of cultural flourishing obscures significant economic and social disruptions caused by his policies, and modern historians debate the extent to which his successor Ienobu actually reversed his policies.
historiographic_note: The year 1715 is exceptionally well-documented for European history, with abundant primary sources from diplomatic, governmental, and personal archives. The death of Louis XIV and the establishment of the Regency are among the most thoroughly documented events in European history. The Jacobite Rising is extensively documented in both government and Jacobite sources, though Jacobite sources may be biased toward justifying their cause. For East Asia, Chinese and Japanese sources are abundant and detailed. However, the history of the Yamasee War is documented almost entirely from the English colonial perspective; Native American accounts are largely absent from surviving written sources, though archaeological and oral historical research has begun to recover Native American perspectives. African, Pacific, and most indigenous American histories for this year remain largely undocumented in available sources. The Regency period in France has been the subject of extensive historiographic debate, with interpretations ranging from a period of moral decline to a period of cultural and intellectual innovation.

graph_edges.note: The Jacobite Rising was a direct response to George I's accession under the Hanoverian succession
Louis XIV's death directly led to the establishment of the Regency
The succession concerns of 1715 culminated in the succession crisis of 1722
Tokugawa Ienobu's early reign coincided with the Treaty of Passarowitz in Europe
